change_summary: Decider self-applied 3 of 5 c3 patches: (1) orphan 'the' regression fix in detail_verdict_text — confirmed gone; (2) Halley century correction — detail_evidence now reads 'Edmund Halley\'s late-17th/early-18th-century declination surveys'; (3) core-dynamics framing alignment — detail_evidence now reads 'core flow and core-mantle differential rotation' (matching tldr_evidence's 'fluid dynamics in Earth\'s outer core'). Two c3 findings (major: templated post-hoc data-source list 'IGRF, NASA station archives, ocean-tide gauges, telluric records, etc.'; moderate: post-hoc paragraph leads with monitor.py absence rather than 390-year public-availability) remain in the live text but are double-tracked in open ISS-1863 (analyst-assigned, multi-WIN scope) and ISS-1866 (analyst-assigned). c3 also surfaced ISS-1865 (defense_survives=3 dome-vs-MHD asymmetric-treatment angle) which is also analyst-assigned and unaddressed in the current text.
our_argument_summary: WIN-060 (Std Model Explains, new_in_v51): westward drift of the SAA western cell is well-tracked by WMM2025/IGRF-14/Swarm; explained by core flow and core-mantle differential rotation; the dome adopts a centuries-old observation as a V51.0 WIN with no derivation of drift rate or direction from dome geometry; same dataset as WIN-040-043, 059-061, 063 and overlaps WIN-004/005, so reading three properties (separation, decay rate, drift direction) from one INTERMAGNET/Swarm dataset does not multiply predictive power.

relevant_files: raw-text/02-wins.txt (V50.6 snapshot, no WIN-060 entry — V51 not in snapshot)
raw-text/03-predictions.txt (W022 SAA western cell position threshold — maps to WIN-040, NOT WIN-060)
raw-text/08-live-power.txt (SAA Decay Rate panel — tracks intensity in nT/yr, not drift direction)
dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: No pipeline. raw-text snapshot (V50.6, 2026-03-12) predates V51.0 introduction of WIN-060 and contains no entry for SAA western cell drift direction. The closest weekly W-test (W022) is a static-position threshold ('West of 45 deg W, cells separated'), not a drift-direction measurement.
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: Inapplicable — no monitor.py entry exists. There is no automated check, no dashboard panel, no API fetch, no comparison logic for SAA westward drift direction in the dome's monitoring suite.
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: None applicable — no automation to gate.
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: None. detail_verdict_text correctly states 'no monitor.py entry exists for this value at all — the dome author has not included it in the monitoring suite.' Confirmed against the V50.6 raw-text snapshot. Caveat: the snapshot is V50.6 (2026-03-12) and the dome is now V51.1; if the dome added a drift-direction monitor in V51 we cannot verify from this raw-text alone. However, the dome's published 'live' panels (08-live-power.txt) only cover intensity, not direction, suggesting no V51 addition either.

code_analysis_tags.monitoring_evidence: Confirmed by direct inspection of raw-text/03-predictions.txt: W022 ('SAA western cell position: West of 45 deg W, cells separated') is a static-threshold check that maps to WIN-040 (position, not drift). raw-text/08-live-power.txt SAA Decay Rate panel tracks intensity (nT/yr), not positional drift. No automated check exists for westward drift direction. (Caveat: raw-text is V50.6 snapshot; not yet refreshed to V51.x. If a V51 drift-direction monitor were added it would not be visible here — but the live-panels structure has not changed in the published V51.x site to our knowledge.)
code_analysis_tags.relabels_standard_evidence: Westward drift of geomagnetic features is standard geophysics — explained by core-mantle differential rotation since Bullard (1950) and quantified by modern MHD core-flow inversions (Finlay et al. 2010, 2016). Detail_evidence now correctly identifies the standard mechanism as 'core flow and core-mantle differential rotation' (per c3 patch). The dome's qualitative 'aetheric rotation' relabel predicts no different numerical drift rate.
code_analysis_tags.post_hoc_evidence: The dome adopts a publicly indexed observation from NOAA WMM2025 / IGRF-14 — both predate the dome's V51.0 release. Westward magnetic drift was first quantified from Henry Gellibrand's 1635 secular-variation discovery; Halley's 1701 Atlantic chart codified it. There is no possibility of a pre-registration of westward drift direction in 2026 because the answer has been public for ~390 years.
code_analysis_tags.derives_from_dome_evidence: No formula on the dome site starts from disc_radius=20015 km or H(r) and arrives at a drift-rate prediction. The dome's 'aetheric rotation' mechanism is qualitative — it cannot reproduce or predict the quantitative MHD core-flow inversions that yield ~0.2 deg/yr westward drift rates.

holes_found.description: OUTSTANDING from c3 (NOT patched): templated post-hoc data-source list still reads '(IGRF, NASA station archives, ocean-tide gauges, telluric records, etc.)' in detail_verdict_text. For a WIN about SAA westward magnetic drift, IGRF is correct, but ocean-tide gauges and telluric records are not relevant data sources. Tracked in open ISS-1863 (assigned-analyst, multi-WIN cleanup scope, found_at 2026-05-06T09:19:37Z). The decider chose to scope this as a per-domain analyst rewrite rather than a singleton patch — a defensible call given the 18-WIN spread, but the live text remains exploitable: a defender screenshotting the WIN-060 panel can ridicule 'why does your post-hoc test for SAA drift mention ocean-tide gauges?' Until the analyst lands the per-domain narrowing, this hole stays open.
holes_found.recommendation: No new action — ISS-1863 is the right vehicle. Counter-argument the operator should weigh: a per-WIN patch on WIN-060 alone (changing the list to 'IGRF-14 / NOAA WMM2025') would close this hole in <1 hour and let the analyst's broader cross-WIN cleanup proceed asynchronously. The risk of waiting is small (the panel is one of 18 and not currently exploited) but non-zero. If the analyst rewrite is >1 week out, escalate.

holes_found.description: OUTSTANDING from c3 (NOT patched): post-hoc paragraph still leads with monitor.py-absence framing rather than the public-availability framing. The current text reads (paraphrased): 'no monitor.py entry exists for this value at all — making even the weaker commit-timestamp test inapplicable.' That gives a defender an opening: 'You penalize us for not monitoring; but the unmonitored claim is still consistent with observation.' Tracked in open ISS-1866 (assigned-analyst). Reordering to lead with '~390 years of public availability since Gellibrand 1635 / Halley 1701; no fresh prediction is possible' would make the argument robust to that defense.
holes_found.recommendation: No new action — ISS-1866 is the right vehicle and the broader monitoring=none post-hoc paragraph applies to ~16 WINs, so the analyst rewrite is the correct scope. Spot-flag for re-verification on next post-rewrite curmudgeon pass.

holes_found.description: OUTSTANDING from c3 (NOT addressed by patches, NOT a c3 finding directly but raised in ISS-1865): defense_survives=3 angle — a dome defender can argue asymmetric treatment of consistency vs derivation. Both dome and standard MHD are 'consistent' with westward drift; only standard MHD derives the rate (~0.2 deg/yr). The detail_verdict_text says 'It has no core-dynamics mechanism to predict drift rate or direction independently' but does not explicitly contrast that against standard MHD's quantitative derivation, leaving the dome defender room to argue 'we are no worse off than the consensus.' Tracked in open ISS-1865.
holes_found.recommendation: No new action — ISS-1865 is the right vehicle. The recommended one-sentence addition ('Both the dome model and standard MHD are consistent with westward drift; only standard MHD derives drift rate quantitatively from first principles, e.g., Finlay 2016 ~0.2 deg/yr from CMB flow inversions') is small enough to consider as a decider singleton patch rather than waiting on analyst rewrite — would close defense_survives=3 without re-running expansion.

holes_found.description: NEW c4 finding: arithmetic inconsistency between '400+ year observational fact' and the now-corrected 'Halley\'s late-17th/early-18th-century declination surveys.' If we anchor to Halley\'s 1701 Atlantic Chart (the most concrete document), 2026−1701 = 325 years, not 400+. Even Halley\'s 1683 paper noting drift gives 343 years. Reaching 400+ requires going back to Henry Gellibrand\'s 1635 secular-variation discovery (391 years) — and even that is just shy of 400. So the prose chains '400+ year observational fact — westward magnetic drift has been documented since Halley\'s late-17th/early-18th-century declination surveys' but the bridge sentence makes the math impossible. A citation-savvy reader could flag this as imprecise. Severity is minor because the qualitative argument (centuries-old, indexed-public) is unaffected; severity is not zero because the c3 review used '~390 years' which is strictly correct against Gellibrand and we now overshot.
holes_found.recommendation: Replace '400+ year observational fact' with either 'three-century observational fact' or '~390-year observational fact' (the latter matches our c3 review prose verbatim). If anchored to the Halley language already in the sentence, '~325-year observational fact' is more conservative still. Trivial decider singleton — under 20 chars edited.

holes_found.description: NEW c4 finding: detail_verdict_text first sentence still says 'It has no core-dynamics mechanism to predict drift rate or direction independently.' The c3 patch updated detail_evidence from 'core dynamics and outer magnetosphere coupling' to the more specific 'core flow and core-mantle differential rotation' but did not echo this update into the verdict text. 'Core-dynamics' as written is broad enough to include core flow and CMB coupling and is not factually wrong — but the inconsistency in specificity (detail uses precise standard-physics term; verdict uses generic shorthand) is mildly distracting and a stylistic regression from the c3 cleanup pass. Verdicts that paraphrase prior detail wording shouldn\'t lag a patch.
holes_found.recommendation: Optional micro-edit: change 'core-dynamics mechanism' to 'core-flow mechanism' in the first sentence of detail_verdict_text. Single-word change. Aligns verdict prose with the (now-correct) detail_evidence. Skip if no other prose touches are queued — not exploitable, just stylistic.

stronger_arguments.description: The strongest defender comeback to our 'no derivation from dome geometry' argument is to point to the dome's 'aetheric rim pressure' framing in WIN-024 (Roaring 40s = SAA Southern Boundary), which DOES posit a geometric coupling between SAA and a disc-edge mechanism. A defender could chain: 'WIN-024 already establishes the SAA is at the disc rim; westward drift is just rim rotation; the prediction IS dome-geometric.' Our verdict text doesn't pre-empt this — we only say 'no derivation,' which is true at the quantitative-rate level but somewhat thin at the qualitative-mechanism level. We could add: 'Even granting the dome\'s qualitative "aetheric rotation" mechanism (cf. WIN-024), it offers no quantitative drift-rate prediction; standard MHD core-flow inversions yield ~0.2 deg/yr from CMB structure (Finlay 2016, Aubert 2014). Qualitative consistency is not a prediction.' This strengthens the argument by acknowledging the kernel-of-truth (rim-pressure intuition exists) and showing why it still doesn\'t save the WIN.
sources: Finlay et al. 2010 'Short timescale core dynamics: theory and observations' Phys. Earth Planet. Inter.
Aubert 2014 'Earth's core internal dynamics 1840-2010 imaged by inverse geodynamo modelling'
WIN-024 detail_evidence in this review (cross-WIN dome rim-pressure framing)

stronger_arguments.description: Quantitative gap argument we could make sharper: standard MHD predicts drift rate ~0.2 deg/yr at the CMB from inverse modelling, and the SAA western cell drifts at roughly that rate (~0.27 deg/yr per Pavón-Carrasco & De Santis 2016). The dome cites the observation but does not claim or derive any rate. Adding a rate-comparison sentence ('standard MHD predicts and observes ~0.2-0.3 deg/yr; the dome predicts no specific rate, so the WIN reduces to claiming the direction is westward, which has been a published fact since 1635') would make the discriminating power difference unambiguous.
sources: Pavón-Carrasco & De Santis 2016 'The South Atlantic Anomaly: The Key for a Possible Geomagnetic Reversal' Frontiers in Earth Science

kernel_of_truth.description: The dome's qualitative claim 'westward drift is consistent with aetheric rotation' has a real kernel: rotational asymmetries in any rotating geophysical system can produce westward drift of magnetic features (data/wins.json:WIN-060.detail_evidence — westward drift is well-documented). The dome's WIN-024 rim-pressure framing also gives the dome a non-trivial geometric story for SAA placement (data/wins.json:WIN-024). Combined, an honest defender can argue the dome at least posits *a* mechanism, even if qualitative.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: Three reasons the kernel doesn't rescue WIN-060: (1) the dome's mechanism is qualitative — no formula starts from disc_radius=20015 km or H(r) and arrives at drift rate (data/wins.json:WIN-060.code_analysis.derives_from_dome=false); (2) the observation predates the dome by ~390 years (Gellibrand 1635, Halley 1701) so adopting it as a 2026 'WIN' is post-hoc by the dome's own definition (data/wins.json:WIN-060.code_analysis.post_hoc=true and the dome author's own pre-registration definition at https://john09289.github.io/predictions); (3) the WIN is one of three properties (separation, decay rate, drift direction) read from the same INTERMAGNET/Swarm dataset across WIN-040-043, 059-061, 063 (data/wins.json:WIN-060.detail_evidence and detail_group field) — single-dataset extrapolation does not multiply predictive power.

deeper_analysis: The c3 patches landed cleanly. The orphan 'the' regression that would have been visible on 16 detail panels is gone (verified by string-search against current data/wins.json). The Halley century framing is now defensible. The core-dynamics→core-flow framing now matches the TLDR. Three separate findings (one major, two moderate) remain unaddressed in the live text but are correctly tracked as open issues assigned to the analyst — this is the system working as designed: cross-WIN cleanups go to analyst rewrites, not piecewise decider patches. The two new minor findings here ('400+ year' arithmetic; 'core-dynamics' lingering in verdict text) are trivial and could be batched with any future touch on this WIN. Verdict (Std Model Explains) holds: westward drift is a 390-year-old indexed observation, the dome offers no quantitative drift-rate prediction, standard MHD does. The strongest open defender angle is ISS-1865's asymmetric-treatment framing (consistency vs derivation); the recommended one-sentence Finlay 2016 citation is small enough that an out-of-band decider singleton patch could close defense_survives=3 without waiting on the analyst's broader rewrite — operator's call.

citations_verified: IGRF-14 https://www.ncei.noaa.gov/products/international-geomagnetic-reference-field — current IGRF generation released 2024-2025, accessible
ESA Swarm L1B https://earth.esa.int/eogateway/missions/swarm — operational mission, L1B data products documented at this URL

Halley's late-17th/early-18th-century declination surveys — historically defensible: Halley's theoretical 1683 paper plus 1698-1700 voyages and 1701 Atlantic Chart span the late-17th/early-18th centuries
citations_unchecked: Part 3.1b carve-out anchor href='#posthoc-standard' — assumed correct, not re-checked against rendered docs/index.html in this run
Dome author's own pre-registration definition quote at john09289.github.io/predictions — assumed correct as quoted in detail_verdict_text, not re-fetched from dome site
